title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Leah Grant, who runs a food truck.
audio_script: Interviewer: Leah Grant is with us today. She runs a food truck that has become very popular in her city. Leah, did you always want to cook for a living? || Guest: I loved cooking, but I planned to work in hotel management. After college I helped in my aunt's café for a few months. She was ill, so I cooked lunches as well as serving customers. [20]When people started coming back especially for my soup, I realised cooking could be my future[/20]. || Interviewer: Why did you choose a food truck rather than a restaurant? || Guest: Money was part of it, of course. Renting a restaurant was too expensive. But that wasn't the main attraction. [21]I liked the idea of moving to different places and meeting different customers every day[/21]. It keeps things interesting. || Interviewer: What was the first year like? || Guest: Very busy. I expected early mornings because food has to be prepared, but I didn't realise how much paperwork there would be. [22]Getting permission to park in the right streets was much harder than learning new recipes[/22]. || Interviewer: Your menu changes every week. How do you decide what to cook? || Guest: I listen to customers, but I don't follow every suggestion. Some people always ask for burgers, which is fine, but lots of places sell those. [23]I usually choose dishes using vegetables that are fresh at the market that morning[/23]. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about your work? || Guest: I enjoy the cooking, obviously, and I like chatting to customers. But [24]the best moment is when someone tastes something unfamiliar and then smiles because they love it[/24]. That's wonderful. || Interviewer: Do you have plans to expand the business? || Guest: People ask if I'll open several trucks, but I don't want to manage staff all day. [25]I'd prefer to write a small cookbook with the simple lunches people can make at home[/25]. I'm already testing recipes for it. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Leah, food trucks are often busy at festivals. Do you enjoy those events? || Guest: I do, but they're exhausting. At a normal lunch stop I can chat to people and explain the food, while at a festival the queue never seems to end. The atmosphere is brilliant, though, especially when music is playing and everyone is relaxed. I take a smaller menu to festivals because people don't want to wait while I prepare something complicated. They want good food quickly, and they want to carry it easily. || Interviewer: Have you made any mistakes that taught you something useful? || Guest: Plenty. Once I cooked far too much of a spicy bean dish because my friends loved it during testing. Customers liked the taste, but it was a hot day and most wanted something lighter. I learned not to plan a menu only around what I enjoy. Weather, location and the time of day all matter. || Interviewer: Do you still cook at home? || Guest: Yes, but very simply. After a day cooking for customers, I don't want to make anything fancy. My favourite meal at home is toast with eggs and tomatoes. People are disappointed when I say that, but it's true. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Leah Grant is with us today. She runs a food truck that has become very popular in her city. Leah, did you always want to cook for a living?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I loved cooking, but I planned to work in hotel management. After college I helped in my aunt's café for a few months. She was ill, so I cooked lunches as well as serving customers. When people started coming back especially for my soup, I realised cooking could be my future.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Why did you choose a food truck rather than a restaurant?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Money was part of it, of course. Renting a restaurant was too expensive. But that wasn't the main attraction. I liked the idea of moving to different places and meeting different customers every day. It keeps things interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was the first year like?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Very busy. I expected early mornings because food has to be prepared, but I didn't realise how much paperwork there would be. Getting permission to park in the right streets was much harder than learning new recipes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Your menu changes every week. How do you decide what to cook?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I listen to customers, but I don't follow every suggestion. Some people always ask for burgers, which is fine, but lots of places sell those. I usually choose dishes using vegetables that are fresh at the market that morning.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about your work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I enjoy the cooking, obviously, and I like chatting to customers. But the best moment is when someone tastes something unfamiliar and then smiles because they love it. That's wonderful.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you have plans to expand the business?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People ask if I'll open several trucks, but I don't want to manage staff all day. I'd prefer to write a small cookbook with the simple lunches people can make at home. I'm already testing recipes for it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Leah, food trucks are often busy at festivals. Do you enjoy those events?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I do, but they're exhausting. At a normal lunch stop I can chat to people and explain the food, while at a festival the queue never seems to end. The atmosphere is brilliant, though, especially when music is playing and everyone is relaxed. I take a smaller menu to festivals because people don't want to wait while I prepare something complicated. They want good food quickly, and they want to carry it easily.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you made any mistakes that taught you something useful?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Plenty. Once I cooked far too much of a spicy bean dish because my friends loved it during testing. Customers liked the taste, but it was a hot day and most wanted something lighter. I learned not to plan a menu only around what I enjoy. Weather, location and the time of day all matter.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you still cook at home?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but very simply. After a day cooking for customers, I don't want to make anything fancy. My favourite meal at home is toast with eggs and tomatoes. People are disappointed when I say that, but it's true.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: What made Leah think about cooking as a career?

options.B: working with her aunt in a café

questions.explanation: Leah says people came back especially for her soup, which made her realise cooking could be her future.

questions.question: Why did Leah choose to run a food truck?

options.A: She liked moving around the city.
options.B: She could not find a suitable restaurant.
options.C: She wanted to save money on staff.

questions.explanation: She says she liked the idea of moving to different places and meeting different customers.

questions.question: What did Leah find especially difficult in the first year?
questions.explanation: Leah says getting permission to park in the right streets was harder than learning recipes.

questions.question: How does Leah usually choose dishes for her menu?
questions.explanation: She says she usually chooses dishes using vegetables that are fresh at the market that morning.

questions.question: What does Leah enjoy most about her work?
questions.explanation: Leah says the best moment is when someone tastes something unfamiliar and smiles because they love it.

questions.question: What does Leah hope to do in the future?
questions.explanation: She says she would prefer to write a small cookbook with simple lunches.
